content:
After years of building and leading engineering teams — from individual contributor to Senior Engineering Manager — I have landed on a management philosophy that I wish someone had handed me earlier. It is not complicated. But it is intentional.
Start with trust, not process #Most management frameworks lead with process: standups, retros, planning ceremonies, ticket hygiene. I lead with trust.
Process without trust is just overhead. Engineers will comply with your ceremonies and route around your intent. But when engineers trust their manager — when they believe you have their back, that you will not weaponize their vulnerabilities, that you will fight for them when it matters — the process becomes almost secondary. They show up differently. They take risks. They tell you the truth early, before problems metastasize.
Building trust is not complicated either. Show up consistently. Do what you say you will do. Protect your team when the pressure is external. Hold them accountable when it is internal. Never surprise them with feedback they have not already heard in private. Be the same person in a 1:1 as you are in an executive review.
That is the whole thing. Trust is not a program. It is a pattern.
Set clear expectations, then get out of the way #Micromanagement is almost always a symptom of unclear expectations. If you have not told an engineer what “great” looks like, you end up watching their work too closely because you have no other signal.
The fix is to front-load clarity. Before someone starts a project, before a quarter kicks off, before a new hire finishes onboarding — invest the time to make the expectations explicit. What does success look like? What does failure look like? Where is the bar? What decisions can they make on their own, and which ones should they loop you in on?
Once that is clear, step back. Talented engineers do not need a manager watching over their shoulder. They need the goal, the context, and the runway.
Recognize strengths and lean into them #Every engineer on a team has something they are exceptional at. The manager’s job is to find it — sometimes before they have found it themselves — and then build the conditions where that strength gets used.
I am not talking about painting everyone’s weakness as a hidden strength. I am talking about genuinely identifying what each person brings to the team and building around it. The engineer who is brilliant at systems thinking gets pulled into architecture conversations. The engineer who communicates exceptionally well becomes the face of the team in cross-functional discussions. The engineer who codes fast and clean gets to set the technical bar on a critical path.
Strength-based management is not just good for morale. It produces better outcomes. People do their best work when they are doing work that plays to who they are.
Be the safety net #The most important thing I do as a manager is make sure my team knows I have their back.
This means that when they make a mistake — and they will, because that is what people do when they are taking real risks and doing real work — the first question they ask is not “will I get in trouble?” but “how do we fix this?”
It means that when organizational pressure comes from above or from peers, I absorb what I can so my team can stay focused.
It means that when someone on my team is struggling — with the work, with a colleague, with something in their life — they feel safe enough to tell me.
None of this means I shield people from accountability. Clear expectations mean there is accountability built in. But accountability and safety are not opposites. The best teams I have built had both: people knew the bar, and they knew someone was in their corner while they tried to clear it.
Zero tolerance for politics #I genuinely do not care whose team did what. “My team vs your team” thinking is one of the most corrosive forces in any engineering organization, and I work hard to excise it wherever it shows up.
The engineers and managers I respect most are the ones who make the whole org better, not just their corner of it. Who share credit, flag issues that are not technically their problem, and treat cross-functional partners like collaborators rather than obstacles.
I try to model this. I try to hire for it. And I try to call it out — directly, in the moment — when I see it happening around me.
Trust the engineers closest to the code #I came up as a software engineer. I can hold a design conversation, read a post-mortem, and tell when an architecture decision smells wrong. But I have never confused that fluency with expertise.
The engineers closest to the codebase know things I do not. They know which parts of the system are fragile. They know which technical debt is actually fine and which is a ticking clock. They know what the junior engineer on their left is struggling with and what the principal engineer on their right is excited about.
My job is to stay informed enough to ask good questions and make good resourcing decisions — not to pretend I have the technical answers. I lean on my Principal Engineers for direction, and I trust my senior engineers to own the decisions within that direction.
That trust is not blind. It is earned incrementally, the same way all trust is. But the default is trust, not skepticism. And that default changes everything.
The short version #If I had to put my whole management philosophy on a sticky note:
Set clear expectations. Build trust before you build anything else. Find each person’s strength and use it. Have their back. Stay out of the politics. Trust the engineers closest to the work.
Everything else is tactics.
This is the first post in a series on engineering leadership. Next up: how I think about merging two engineering teams without losing either culture.
summary:
After years of building and leading engineering teams — from individual contributor to Senior Engineering Manager — I have landed on a management philosophy that I wish someone had handed me earlier. It is not complicated. But it is intentional.

Start with trust, not process #

Most management frameworks lead with process: standups, retros, planning ceremonies, ticket hygiene. I lead with trust.
title: The Management Philosophy I Wish Someone Had Told Me

content:
Who I am #I build teams where engineers feel safe, trusted, and empowered to do their best work.
My management philosophy is straightforward: set clear expectations, earn trust over time, and make sure every person on my team knows I have their back — unconditionally. I don’t believe in micromanagement. Talented engineers already know how to solve hard problems. My job is to remove obstacles, protect the team from organizational noise, and create the space where they can focus.
I lean on my Principal Engineers for technical direction and trust my senior engineers to own their decisions. I ask good questions and stay informed — but I don’t pretend to have all the answers. I think that’s a feature, not a bug.
I have zero patience for organizational politics. “My team vs your team” thinking is a culture killer. I push hard for collaboration across boundaries and try to be the manager who lifts others rather than one who hoards credit or territory.
Currently at TiVo, I lead engineering delivery across Android, iOS, and Roku for our PayTV streaming platform — managing multiple teams including an offshore contractor relationship on Roku.
Domain expertise # Multi-platform streaming — Android, iOS, Roku, Web, LG & Samsung SmartTVs Live video streaming & OTT — PayTV platform delivery at scale Programmatic ad insertion — client-side and server-side ad tech Engineering efficiency — delivery metrics, process improvement, tooling Team culture & org design — mergers, onboarding, psychological safety Offshore & contractor management — delivery models, accountability structures Signature strengths #People-first leadership #I build trust before I build process. Engineers on my teams know they can bring me any problem without it becoming a performance conversation. That safety net is what unlocks honest communication — which is what unlocks great work.
Multi-platform delivery breadth #Across TiVo and MobiTV, I have owned delivery across Android, iOS, Roku, Web, and SmartTV platforms simultaneously. I understand what it means to ship on different release cycles, different store review processes, and different device ecosystems — and how to keep multiple teams aligned across all of them.
Cross-functional bridge-builder #I get pulled into conversations between product, engineering, and business stakeholders when alignment is breaking down. I translate well in both directions and have a low tolerance for “not my problem” energy from any side of the table.
Technical depth with smart delegation #I came up as a software engineer and still engage actively in architecture reviews, design discussions, and incident post-mortems. I know enough to ask the right questions — and I know when to step back and trust the engineers who live in the codebase every day.
Career arc #Senior Engineering Manager — TiVo #2021 – Present
Leading Android, iOS, and Roku app engineering for TiVo’s PayTV streaming platform. Managing multiple teams including a direct engineering org and an offshore contractor relationship on Roku. Responsible for development and delivery across TiVo’s largest streaming user surfaces.
Senior Technical Program Manager — MobiTV (acquired by TiVo) #2019 – 2021
Drove delivery across Android, iOS, Web, Roku, and SmartTV (LG & Samsung) platforms. Managed cross-platform release coordination and technical program execution through MobiTV’s acquisition by TiVo — a complex organizational transition that sharpened my instincts on team culture and change management.
Senior Technical Program Manager — Bridgewater Associates #2018 – 2019
Led enhancement projects for Bridgewater’s in-house trading platform. Worked at the intersection of engineering, quantitative research, and operations in one of the most rigorous, documentation-driven environments in the industry.
Technical Project Manager / Scrum Master — AIG #2017 – 2018
Drove in-house modernization initiatives across AIG’s engineering organization. Managed delivery teams through complex legacy system transformations.
Technical Project Manager / Scrum Master — TIAA #2014 – 2017
Led projects across Banking & Trust — including a hands-on migration of automation frameworks to Selenium and a major Trust Platform migration. Worked closely with engineering teams on test strategy and delivery sequencing.
Staff Engineer / Delivery — Bank of America #2012 – 2014
Web developer and delivery lead on Credit Card Application modernization. Bridging engineering and delivery here was an early signal of the engineering management path ahead.
Engineer → Senior Engineer — Bank of America / Kohls #2006 – 2012
Where it all started. Built the engineering fundamentals — frontend development, delivery basics, working in large organizations — that still inform how I think about the craft today.
Get in touch #Always happy to connect with other engineering leaders, swap notes on streaming tech, or talk shop on team building and culture.
LinkedIn GitHub Email
summary:
Who I am #

I build teams where engineers feel safe, trusted, and empowered to do their best work.

My management philosophy is straightforward: set clear expectations, earn trust over time, and make sure every person on my team knows I have their back — unconditionally. I don’t believe in micromanagement. Talented engineers already know how to solve hard problems. My job is to remove obstacles, protect the team from organizational noise, and create the space where they can focus.
title: About
